narrative: As an Asian male applicant with a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.90—well above the typical 3.21 average—and a solid LSAT score of 40, which exceeds the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many law school hopefuls would envy. However, your application for admission was rejected because the model's prediction hinges on estimating your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it calculated a 43.9% probability that you would fail. This threshold triggered the denial, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to succeed on that critical exam right away. Among all the factors considered, like your lower-middle family income bracket, full-time student status, and Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles of 3 and 2 respectively (both below averages of about 5.5-5.7), three stood out as having the strongest sway in this decision. Your LSAT score was the most influential, working strongly in your favor by pushing the prediction toward expecting you to pass the bar. Next, your Year 3 decile rank pulled notably in the opposite direction, signaling to the model a heightened risk of failure based on that relatively lower performance compared to peers. Your undergraduate GPA followed as the third key factor, providing a meaningful boost toward a passing prediction thanks to its strength above the norm. Overall, while your test scores and college record give you an edge over typical applicants, the dip in your later high school decile tipped the balance enough to raise concerns about bar exam success. This doesn't reflect your full potential or personal drive, especially as someone from an Asian background where academic pressures can be intense, but rather how these metrics stacked up against patterns in past students. To move forward, consider retaking or bolstering any weaker academic areas, like seeking targeted prep for metrics tied to your Year 3 performance, and reapply after building even stronger evidence of consistency. You might also explore programs with holistic reviews or appeal with additional context about your journey. With your standout GPA and LSAT already setting you apart, targeted improvements could easily shift the odds in your favor next time. Keep pushing—you're closer than this one prediction suggests.
